channel.title: Embedded Linux on Tony He Embedded Systems Lab
channel.description: Recent content in Embedded Linux on Tony He Embedded Systems Lab

item.title: Buildroot Minimal Linux System
item.description: Buildroot is useful when a product needs a compact Linux system with predictable packages and a short build path. It is often a good fit for gateways, display controllers, appliance interfaces, and single-purpose industrial devices. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Device Tree Display and GPIO Review
item.description: Device tree review is one of the highest value steps in an embedded Linux project. It connects the schematic to the kernel driver model and often explains why a display stays blank, a reset line toggles at the wrong time, or a peripheral never probes. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Linux Kernel Driver Debug Notes
item.description: Kernel driver debugging becomes easier when the driver, device tree, clocks, regulators, interrupts, and runtime logs are reviewed together. A failed probe is usually a structured dependency problem, not a random kernel behavior. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Rockchip BSP Bring-up Notes
item.description: Rockchip BSP work usually starts from a vendor tree that already boots on a reference board. The engineering task is to turn that baseline into a reliable product image for a specific carrier board, display, storage device, and production process. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: U-Boot Debugging for Custom Boards
item.description: U-Boot is the first software layer that most board teams can observe and modify. A clean bootloader debug method separates power, strap, DRAM, storage, environment, and kernel handoff problems instead of treating every failure as a single boot issue. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Yocto Board Customization Workflow
item.description: Yocto is valuable when a product needs long-term maintainability, layered customization, package control, and reproducible images across board revisions. The challenge is not only building an image but keeping board changes organized over time. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.
